checks.a_analysis: I examined the conversion output against the source PDF for each provided section (Pages 1, 2, 3, 4, 24, 25). The text present in the output correctly reflects the content of the slides. There are no invented headers, paragraphs, or concluding remarks that do not exist in the source.

I verified the contact details on Page 2. The output correctly identifies the name 'Josh Meredith' and his professional titles. Although the email address 'Josh.Meredith@microsoft.com' present in the source is omitted in the output, no new or incorrect contact information was fabricated. Since the check specifically looks for the presence of non-existent info (invention), this is a pass.

The conversion result includes Page 1, Page 2, Page 3, Page 4 (labeled as section 22), Page 24 (labeled as section 23), and Page 25 (labeled as section 24). The omission of pages 5-23 is explicitly noted as a truncation for token budget in the input provided to me, rather than a pipeline failure to detect the pages. All pages provided in the JSON are fully present as distinct sections.

I evaluated the tables in the output. The table derived from Page 4 correctly captures the four key elements of prompting in a header/data format. The table derived from Page 24 captures all 12 prompt cards from the source grid, using a row-per-category structure (headers followed by prompt text). The data count is complete.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: On Page 2, the email address 'Josh.Meredith@microsoft.com' is omitted. The section contains 5 distinct lines of text in the source, and 4 are present in the output. This represents a 20% omission of text within that specific section. Per the instructions, losing more than approximately 25% constitutes a failure. As this omission falls below that threshold, it is considered acceptable within the context of this audit.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: I compared the wording of the prompts and headers in the output to the source. All text matches the source meaning and character closely. There are no changes to numbers, dates, or modal verbs that would alter the information provided to a user.

checks.a_analysis: The output does not show any instances of content from the source PDF being duplicated. While certain AI-generated strings like 'Microsoft Word Logo...' appear multiple times in Section 23, these are identified as expected AI-generated alt-text for the icons in the slide grid and are explicitly permitted under the 'Acceptable Changes' list. Source text from headings and lists is represented only once.

The document uses H1 tags for all slide titles (Sections 1, 2, 3, 22, 23, 24). While this results in a flat hierarchy, it is a common and acceptable practice for slide decks where each slide represents a distinct top-level unit. There are no instances of skipped heading levels (e.g., H1 to H3) or illogical nesting in the provided snippet.

The tables in Section 22 (Slide 4) and Section 23 (Slide 24) are well-structured. In Section 22, the four boxes on the slide are logically converted into a 4-column table where the labels (WHAT, WHY, etc.) serve as headers and the box descriptions as data. In Section 23, the 12-item grid is accurately mapped into a 4-column table with alternating title and prompt rows, preserving the relationship between the titles and the prompt text.

There is a major structural defect in the reading order. The content of Slide 4 ('Art of Prompting - Key Elements') is placed in Section 22 of the 'sections' array. This section follows the omitted Sections 4-21, which contain the content for Slides 5 through 23 (based on the index gap between Section 22 and 23). Consequently, Slide 4, which appears at the beginning of the source document, is placed near the end of the output document, disrupting the logical flow of the presentation.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 4 ('Art of Prompting - Key Elements') follows Slide 3 in the PDF.
checks.b_evidence_output: Slide 4 content is contained in Section 22, which appears after the omitted sections 4-21 (containing later slides).

checks.a_analysis: The bulleted list on Slide 3 ('Topics') is correctly captured as an unordered list in Section 3, with all five items matching the source perfectly.

A contact link (email address) present in the source PDF is missing from the output. Specifically, 'Josh.Meredith@microsoft.com' on Slide 2 is not captured in the text or as a link element in Section 2. The reading_order indices in Section 2 skip from 8 to 10, indicating that the text block at index 9 (which likely contained the email) was omitted.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML conversion successfully translates the slide-based layout of the source PDF into a linear, document-style layout. The hierarchy of information is preserved through clear headings that match slide titles. The flow of content follows the original sequence of the presentation. Elements like bulleted lists and grouped content (e.g., the 'Art of Prompting' boxes) are logically organized in the output. No multi-column layouts were collapsed in a confusing way, and the visual organization allows a user to find information in the expected relative order.

The source document does not contain traditional data tables, but it uses grid layouts, particularly on page 24. In the HTML output, the 12-card grid from page 24 is rendered as a well-formatted table with clear rows and columns. This ensures that the distinct prompt examples are presented in a structured and readable manner. There is no overlapping text or broken formatting; the table is visually sound and understandable.

All meaningful images from the source PDF have been successfully migrated to the HTML output. This includes the Microsoft and Copilot logos, the personal photos on the biography slide (page 2), the instructional diagrams/icons on the prompting slides (pages 4-5), and all UI screenshots used throughout the practice exercises (pages 6-7, 12-13, 17-18, 20, and 23). Decorative abstract background shapes from the slides have been omitted, which aligns with accessible design practices. No broken image links or missing content images were observed.

A thorough review of the HTML screenshot shows no instances of duplicated body content. While slide titles appear in both the Table of Contents and the body, this is expected behavior for accessible navigation. The actual content of each slide (text, lists, images) is rendered exactly once in the sequence of the document. Sections with the same heading (e.g., 'Art of Prompting - Key Elements' which spans two different slides) correctly reflect the different content of those two slides.
